metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix AC item 3 to consistently call JIRA_URL and JIRA_USER 'repo variables' (not secrets), matching the What section's explicit 'repo VARIABLES, not secrets' designation, so the operator attests to the correct GitHub configuration type.
findings.finding: AC item 3 labels JIRA_URL and JIRA_USER as 'secrets' while the What section explicitly designates them as 'repo VARIABLES, not secrets' (sourced via vars.* not secrets.*). An implementer following AC item 3 verbatim would configure them as GitHub secrets and the workflow's `vars.JIRA_URL` / `vars.JIRA_USER` references would silently resolve to empty strings, causing the live Jira tests to keep skipping.
findings.location: AC item 3 vs What item 1
findings.suggested_fix: Change AC item 3 to: 'The JIRA_URL and JIRA_USER repo variables, the JIRA_API_TOKEN secret, and the ACLI_VERSION/ACLI_SHA256 repo variables are configured in the GitHub repo ...' — mirroring the What section's exact distinction.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an operator-attested AC (parallel to AC3) requiring a post-provisioning manual `workflow_dispatch` trigger with evidence that ≥1 external test executed (not all skipped), confirming the acli auth and Jira env wiring are live.
findings.finding: No acceptance criterion exercises the newly wired scheduled workflow end-to-end against real Jira (a real triggered run confirming ≥1 live test executed and the run is green). The config-as-artifact test verifies YAML structure, and the behavioral hook test is offline — neither proves the acli auth step works, the Jira creds reach the test, or the live round-trip executes. The whole motivation of the plan is to detect when the real integration is broken, but no AC proves the new path was ever exercised in practice.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria §1 and §3; What §1 (acli install + auth steps)
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: '[operator-attested] After secrets/variables are configured (AC3), manually trigger `external-integration.yml` via `workflow_dispatch` and confirm the run completes with ≥1 external test in PASSED or FAILED state (not all SKIPPED) — attach the run URL as evidence. This proves the acli install, PATH, and Jira auth steps actually work end-to-end.'

findings.finding: The plan claims the reused acli install step is 'sha256-checked', but the step in `reconcile-bridge.yml` (lines 156–159) only emits a `::warning::` and continues if `ACLI_SHA256` is unset — it does NOT fail. If `ACLI_SHA256` is not provisioned as a repo variable, the scheduled canary silently installs an unverified binary, undermining the supply-chain integrity the plan intends.
findings.location: What §1 / AC line 1
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan's What §1, change 'sha256-checked' to 'sha256-checked (ACLI_SHA256 must be set; the step fails if absent)' and add a pre-flight check analogous to the ACLI_VERSION guard at reconcile-bridge.yml lines 150–153 that exits non-zero when ACLI_SHA256 is unset.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the plan's description of the 'Add acli to PATH and authenticate' step to match reconcile-bridge.yml line 181 (`sudo ln -sf "$HOME/.acli/acli" /usr/local/bin/acli`), not a $GITHUB_PATH append, so the implementation and config-as-artifact test are keyed to the actual source.
findings.finding: The plan misstates how reconcile-bridge.yml puts acli on PATH. The plan says the 'Add acli to PATH and authenticate' step appends '~/.acli' to '$GITHUB_PATH', but the actual reconcile-bridge.yml step (line 181) uses 'sudo ln -sf "$HOME/.acli/acli" /usr/local/bin/acli' — a symlink into /usr/local/bin, not a $GITHUB_PATH append. Implementers copying 'the step verbatim in shape' from the plan description will get the wrong implementation; the config-as-artifact test's assertion about 'acli PATH step' may also be keyed to the wrong mechanism.
findings.location: What / Item 1 — 'Add acli to PATH and authenticate' step description; reconcile-bridge.yml line 181
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'append ~/.acli to $GITHUB_PATH' with 'symlink ~/.acli/acli into /usr/local/bin via sudo ln -sf (matching reconcile-bridge.yml line 181)' in the What section and AC bullet.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the What item 1 description: the `Add acli to PATH and authenticate` step uses `sudo ln -sf "$HOME/.acli/acli" /usr/local/bin/acli` (symlink into /usr/local/bin), not `$GITHUB_PATH` appending — update the plan prose to match reconcile-bridge.yml's actual convention.
findings.finding: The plan's What item 1 describes the `Add acli to PATH` step as 'append `~/.acli` to `$GITHUB_PATH`', but the actual reconcile-bridge.yml convention uses `sudo ln -sf "$HOME/.acli/acli" /usr/local/bin/acli` — not `$GITHUB_PATH` appending. The plan says to reuse reconcile-bridge's step 'verbatim in shape', creating a contradiction between the description and the cited reference. An implementer following the prose rather than the reference file will produce a non-matching implementation.
findings.location: What item 1 / .github/workflows/reconcile-bridge.yml lines 174-185
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'append ~/.acli to $GITHUB_PATH' with 'symlink the binary into /usr/local/bin via `sudo ln -sf "$HOME/.acli/acli" /usr/local/bin/acli`' in What item 1.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the contradiction between 'verbatim in shape' and the description of the PATH step: either (a) specify that the implementer should copy the `sudo ln -sf ...` form from reconcile-bridge.yml exactly, or (b) explicitly state that the $GITHUB_PATH variant is an intentional deviation and why it is preferred.
findings.finding: The plan describes the 'Add acli to PATH and authenticate' step as 'append `~/.acli` to `$GITHUB_PATH`', but the actual reconcile-bridge.yml step (lines 174-185) does NOT use $GITHUB_PATH at all — it runs `sudo ln -sf "$HOME/.acli/acli" /usr/local/bin/acli`. The plan claims to reuse these steps 'verbatim in shape', creating a contradiction between the directive ('verbatim in shape') and the description of the mechanism ('append ~/.acli to $GITHUB_PATH'). An implementer following the prose description would produce a step that diverges from the source, and the plan provides no rationale for which variant to use.
findings.location: What §1, bullet 'the `Add acli to PATH and authenticate` step'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'append `~/.acli` to `$GITHUB_PATH`' with 'symlink acli into /usr/local/bin via `sudo ln -sf "$HOME/.acli/acli" /usr/local/bin/acli`' to match the actual reconcile-bridge.yml step verbatim.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the plan's description of the 'Add acli to PATH and authenticate' step to match reconcile-bridge.yml's actual mechanism (`sudo ln -sf "$HOME/.acli/acli" /usr/local/bin/acli`) rather than a `$GITHUB_PATH` append, and ensure the config-as-artifact test pins the symlink form.
findings.finding: The plan describes the 'Add acli to PATH and authenticate' step as appending '~/.acli' to '$GITHUB_PATH', but the actual reconcile-bridge.yml it cites as the authoritative source uses 'sudo ln -sf "$HOME/.acli/acli" /usr/local/bin/acli' (a symlink into /usr/local/bin) — not a $GITHUB_PATH append. This internal contradiction between the plan's prose and the cited prior art means an implementer following the plan text will diverge from reconcile-bridge's actual shape, which also undermines the config-as-artifact test's ability to pin the correct step.
findings.location: What > item 1 (acli install) — 'Add acli to PATH and authenticate' step description; reconcile-bridge.yml lines 174-185
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'append ~/.acli to $GITHUB_PATH' in the plan with 'symlink the acli binary into /usr/local/bin via sudo ln -sf "$HOME/.acli/acli" /usr/local/bin/acli', matching reconcile-bridge.yml line 181 verbatim.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the 'Add acli to PATH and authenticate' step description to match the actual reconcile-bridge.yml mechanism: `sudo ln -sf "$HOME/.acli/acli" /usr/local/bin/acli` (symlink to /usr/local/bin), not `echo ~/.acli >> $GITHUB_PATH`; update the config-as-artifact test assertion accordingly.
findings.finding: The plan's 'What' section describes the 'Add acli to PATH and authenticate' step as 'append ~/.acli to $GITHUB_PATH', but the actual reconcile-bridge.yml it instructs implementors to mirror 'verbatim in shape' uses `sudo ln -sf "$HOME/.acli/acli" /usr/local/bin/acli` (a symlink to /usr/local/bin) — never $GITHUB_PATH. An implementor following the plan's prose will produce a step that diverges from the stated authoritative template, and any config-as-artifact test written to assert the $GITHUB_PATH mechanism will test the wrong thing.
findings.location: What section, item 1 — 'Add acli to PATH and authenticate' step description; reconcile-bridge.yml lines 179-185
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section item 1, replace 'append ~/.acli to $GITHUB_PATH' with 'sudo ln -sf "$HOME/.acli/acli" /usr/local/bin/acli' to match reconcile-bridge.yml line 181 exactly. In the config-as-artifact test, assert the YAML contains 'ln -sf' or '/usr/local/bin/acli', not $GITHUB_PATH.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact pytest hook primitive the fail-on-all-skip canary uses to accumulate counts (e.g. `pytest_runtest_logreport` filtering `report.when == 'call'`), where its counter state lives (e.g. a `pytest_configure`-registered plugin class), and confirm it correctly captures autouse-fixture-level skips from `_require_external_opt_in`.
findings.finding: The fail-on-all-skip hook's counter accumulation mechanism is never specified: the plan names a 'session hook' that 'REPORTS executed-vs-skipped counts' and 'FAILS if EVERY external test skipped (zero executed)', but does not name which pytest hook primitive accumulates the count (e.g. pytest_runtest_logreport, pytest_runtest_makereport, or pytest_sessionfinish querying session attributes), nor where the running counter state lives (module-level mutable, plugin class, session.stash). The existing tests/external/conftest.py has no counting infrastructure. Crucially, the _require_external_opt_in autouse fixture issues pytest.skip() at fixture setup time — whether these skips appear in the report-hook's 'skipped' tally vs. being transparent to a sessionfinish counter is implementation-defined and unspecified in the plan. An implementor has no authoritative placement, risking a canary that silently never fires (wrong hook primitive) or double-counts skips (fixture-scope vs. test-scope).
findings.location: What / item 2: 'Fail-on-all-skip canary (tests/external/conftest.py)'
findings.suggested_fix: Name `pytest_runtest_logreport(report)` as the accumulator hook (fires for every test outcome including autouse-skipped ones at call phase), storing `executed` and `skipped` counts on a `pytest_configure`-registered plugin object, and invoke `pytest.exit(..., returncode=1)` from `pytest_sessionfinish` when `executed == 0 and REBAR_RUN_EXTERNAL` is truthy.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the pytest mechanism for failing the session (e.g., 'raise `pytest.UsageError` in `pytest_sessionfinish`, or set `session.exitstatus = pytest.ExitCode.TESTS_FAILED`') so the behavioral test has a concrete, verifiable observable to assert against.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that the conftest hook 'FAILS the session' but does not specify the pytest mechanism — `pytest_sessionfinish` with `session.exitstatus`, `pytest.exit()`, injecting a synthetic failure item, or raising an exception. The behavioral test will need to verify a specific observable outcome (exit code? collected item?), and without a stated mechanism the executor and the test author may choose incompatible approaches.
findings.location: What §2 ('FAILS the session if EVERY external test skipped') and AC2
findings.suggested_fix: Add to What §2: 'Implement as a `pytest_sessionfinish` hook that sets `session.exitstatus = pytest.ExitCode.TESTS_FAILED` when the all-skip condition is met; the behavioral test verifies `session.exitstatus` equals `TESTS_FAILED` in the all-skip case and `OK` (or unchanged) otherwise.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the exact weekly cron expression (day, hour, timezone offset) in the plan so the config-as-artifact test pins an explicitly chosen value rather than an executor-invented one.
findings.finding: The plan says the workflow should fire 'weekly' but never states the specific cron expression (day of week, hour, minute). The config-as-artifact test will need to assert a concrete cron string, so the executor must invent one — a defaultable gap that should be a stated default in the plan.
findings.location: What §1 ('on.schedule cron trigger (weekly)') and AC1 ('A config-as-artifact test pins the schedule')
findings.suggested_fix: Add e.g. `schedule: [{cron: '0 6 * * 1'}]` (Monday 06:00 UTC) to the What §1 description and echo it in AC1 so the artifact test has a canonical value to assert.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether the fail-on-all-skip hook should apply to the langfuse-trace job's single-test invocation (where an all-skip can legitimately mean the Langfuse stack failed, not missing creds), and either scope the hook to exclude that path or document the intended behaviour.
findings.finding: The plan's fail-on-all-skip hook is added to tests/external/conftest.py, which is session-scoped and applies to BOTH jobs in external-integration.yml that set REBAR_RUN_EXTERNAL=1: the 'external' job (pytest over all of tests/external/) AND the 'langfuse-trace' job (pytest only over tests/external/test_llm_trace.py). The langfuse-trace job runs a single focused test; if that test executes (not skips), the hook is a no-op. But if the Langfuse stack fails to come up and test_llm_trace.py skips, the hook would fire and fail the langfuse-trace job with a misleading 'all external tests skipped' error rather than surfacing the actual Langfuse infra failure. The plan does not address this interaction.
findings.location: What / Item 2 — fail-on-all-skip canary description; external-integration.yml langfuse-trace job (line 153)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether the fail-on-all-skip hook should also apply to the `langfuse-trace` job's single-test pytest session (which also sets `REBAR_RUN_EXTERNAL=1`), and if not, specify how the hook is scoped to avoid a false-fail there.
findings.finding: The `langfuse-trace` job in `external-integration.yml` is an unenumerated consumer of the new fail-on-all-skip conftest hook. That job also sets `REBAR_RUN_EXTERNAL: "1"` and runs `pytest -m external tests/external/test_llm_trace.py` — so the hook fires in that session too. If `test_llm_trace.py` skips (e.g., missing tracing/agents extra or no LANGFUSE_HOST), the hook would fail the `langfuse-trace` job. The plan does not acknowledge this interaction or specify whether the hook should apply to single-test sub-sessions.
findings.location: .github/workflows/external-integration.yml (langfuse-trace job) / What item 2 / tests/external/conftest.py

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether the fail-on-all-skip canary is scoped to full-suite external sessions only (e.g. gated on an additional env var or by counting collected items > N), or document that it intentionally applies to all external sessions including the single-test langfuse-trace job and explain why that is acceptable.
findings.finding: The decision rule 'FAILS if EVERY external test skipped' is not co-located with a specification of which pytest sessions it applies to: the langfuse-trace job in external-integration.yml also sets REBAR_RUN_EXTERNAL=1 and runs a single external test (test_llm_trace.py) that skips unless the [agents]+[tracing] extras AND LANGFUSE_HOST are all present. The plan scopes the canary to 'the external-tier' globally, but the hook lives in tests/external/conftest.py which activates for ALL sessions that collect from tests/external/ — including the langfuse-trace job's narrow single-test session. If that one test skips (plausible: stack startup lag, missing extra), the canary will fail the langfuse-trace job even though that is unrelated to Jira drift. The plan never specifies whether the canary should apply to all external sessions or only the 'external' job that runs the full suite.
findings.location: What / item 2 + .github/workflows/external-integration.yml (langfuse-trace job, lines 153-156)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the Why section to acknowledge that _live_jira_ready() is independently defined in four external test files (not just test_link_sync_roundtrip_live.py), so the operator-attested AC covers the full set of newly-enabled live Jira tests.
findings.finding: The plan references only test_link_sync_roundtrip_live.py::_live_jira_ready() as the gating function, but four separate test files each independently define their own copy of _live_jira_ready() (test_link_sync_roundtrip_live.py, test_link_sync_live.py, test_reconcile_live.py, test_verified_fake_contract_live.py). The fix (adding JIRA_* env + acli to external-integration.yml) unblocks all four, but the plan's scope and canary discussion only acknowledges one test. The fail-on-all-skip hook's correctness claim ('Jira tests still skip') relies on there being one gating point, but actually there are four independent ones — all four would become unblocked by the plan's changes.
findings.location: Why section — '`test_link_sync_roundtrip_live.py::_live_jira_ready()` gates on BOTH'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Scope the all-skip canary hook so it does not false-fire on the langfuse-trace job's scheduled invocation: either (a) exempt test_llm_trace.py from the count (since its skip is expected when no Langfuse stack is provisioned), (b) add a separate opt-in env var (e.g. `REBAR_REQUIRE_JIRA`) that the hook checks instead of the generic `REBAR_RUN_EXTERNAL`, or (c) document that the langfuse-trace job must also be given a real Langfuse stack on scheduled runs (and specify how).
findings.finding: The all-skip session hook added to tests/external/conftest.py fires for EVERY pytest invocation of tests/external/ when REBAR_RUN_EXTERNAL=1 — including the existing langfuse-trace job in external-integration.yml, which also sets REBAR_RUN_EXTERNAL: '1' and runs pytest against tests/external/test_llm_trace.py. On scheduled runs, no Langfuse stack is provisioned, so that single test always skips (gated on LANGFUSE_HOST/PUBLIC_KEY/SECRET_KEY per lines 74-83 of test_llm_trace.py). The hook would fire 'all skipped' → SESSION FAILED on the langfuse-trace job on every scheduled invocation, even though that outcome is expected. The plan does not address this interaction at all.
findings.location: What §2 (conftest.py fail-on-all-skip hook) + .github/workflows/external-integration.yml langfuse-trace job (lines 153-156)
findings.suggested_fix: In the hook implementation, make the 'fail on all skip' logic conditional on at least one of the Jira env vars being set (JIRA_URL/JIRA_API_TOKEN), or introduce a narrower env var (REBAR_REQUIRE_JIRA_LIVE=1) that the scheduled Jira-enabled run sets, so the hook only fires when a live Jira run is expected — not for the separate langfuse-trace invocation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a doc artifact (CLAUDE.md section, ADR stub, or prominent module-level docstring in `conftest.py`) capturing WHY the fail-on-all-skip hook exists, what it guards against, and that removing it restores the false-green behaviour.
findings.finding: The new `conftest.py` fail-on-all-skip hook introduces a novel CI quality-gate pattern whose rationale (and the reason it must not be silently removed) lives only in the plan text, not in any in-repo artifact.
findings.location: tests/external/conftest.py (What §2 / AC #2)
findings.suggested_fix: At minimum, add a module-level docstring to `tests/external/conftest.py` explaining: 'This hook turns an all-skip external run into a hard failure when REBAR_RUN_EXTERNAL is set, preventing creds/acli misconfiguration from producing a false-green CI run. See docs/decisions/external-canary.md.' Then add a one-paragraph CLAUDE.md entry referencing the pattern.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `timeout-minutes` on the acli auth step (and/or the job) so a hung Jira endpoint fails fast and the canary emits a clear, timely error rather than silently occupying a runner for hours.
findings.finding: The acli `auth login` step in the workflow (which calls an external Jira HTTPS endpoint) has no timeout specified, so a slow or hung Jira endpoint during a scheduled run can block a GitHub Actions runner for up to 6 hours — the platform default — degrading runner pool capacity with no diagnostic signal.
findings.location: What — item 1: 'Add acli to PATH and authenticate' step in external-integration.yml
findings.suggested_fix: Add `timeout-minutes: 5` to the 'Add acli to PATH and authenticate' step, and/or set a job-level `timeout-minutes: 30` on the external-integration job; document that a timeout failure here means Jira infra is unavailable, distinct from test failures.

findings.finding: The plan states that `JIRA_URL`/`JIRA_USER`/`JIRA_API_TOKEN` should be mapped 'from `secrets.*`' into the external job env, but `reconcile-bridge.yml` (the template the plan says to mirror) sources `JIRA_URL` and `JIRA_USER` from `vars.*` (repo Variables) and only `JIRA_API_TOKEN` from `secrets.*`. If the implementer follows the plan literally and uses `secrets.JIRA_URL` / `secrets.JIRA_USER`, the values will be empty unless the operator configures them as secrets instead of variables — breaking the live Jira gate. The operator-attested AC also calls all three 'secrets', compounding the confusion.
findings.location: What § item 1 ('map `JIRA_URL`/`JIRA_USER`/`JIRA_API_TOKEN` from `secrets.*`'); AC line 1 ('JIRA_URL/JIRA_USER/JIRA_API_TOKEN secrets'); AC line 3 (operator-attested, 'secrets')
findings.suggested_fix: In What §1: 'map `JIRA_URL`/`JIRA_USER` from `vars.*` and `JIRA_API_TOKEN` from `secrets.*`'. In AC1 and AC3: replace 'secrets' with 'repo Variables (JIRA_URL, JIRA_USER) and secret (JIRA_API_TOKEN)'.

findings.finding: The plan maps JIRA_URL and JIRA_USER from `secrets.*` but the repo's established convention in `reconcile-bridge.yml` sources them from `vars.JIRA_URL` / `vars.JIRA_USER` (GitHub Actions repository variables, not secrets); only `JIRA_API_TOKEN` is a secret. The AC and the operator-attest AC bullet also treat all three as 'secrets'. If JIRA_URL and JIRA_USER are stored as `vars.*` (the established pattern), the plan's `secrets.JIRA_URL` / `secrets.JIRA_USER` references would silently resolve to empty strings.
findings.location: What §1 ('map `JIRA_URL`/`JIRA_USER`/`JIRA_API_TOKEN` from `secrets.*`'); AC bullet 1 ('maps `JIRA_URL`/`JIRA_USER`/`JIRA_API_TOKEN`'); AC bullet 3 ('the `JIRA_URL`/`JIRA_USER`/`JIRA_API_TOKEN` secrets')
findings.suggested_fix: In the workflow edit, use `JIRA_URL: ${{ vars.JIRA_URL }}`, `JIRA_USER: ${{ vars.JIRA_USER }}`, `JIRA_API_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.JIRA_API_TOKEN }}` — mirroring reconcile-bridge.yml lines 87–89. Update AC bullet 3 to say '`JIRA_URL`/`JIRA_USER` repo variables and `JIRA_API_TOKEN` secret'.

findings.finding: The plan says to map JIRA_URL and JIRA_USER 'from secrets.*' into the workflow env, but the established codebase pattern (reconcile-bridge.yml lines 87-88, 176-177, 209-210) stores JIRA_URL and JIRA_USER as repository VARIABLES (vars.*), not secrets. If the implementer follows the plan's 'secrets.*' wording, these two env vars will be empty on any repo where JIRA_URL/JIRA_USER are correctly stored as variables, silently preserving the all-skip condition the plan is meant to fix.
findings.location: What section, item 1: 'map JIRA_URL/JIRA_USER/JIRA_API_TOKEN from secrets.*'
findings.suggested_fix: In external-integration.yml, use `JIRA_URL: ${{ vars.JIRA_URL }}`, `JIRA_USER: ${{ vars.JIRA_USER }}`, and `JIRA_API_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.JIRA_API_TOKEN }}`; update AC1 and AC3 to reflect the vars/secrets split.

findings.finding: The plan maps JIRA_URL and JIRA_USER from `secrets.*` but the repo's established prior art (reconcile-bridge.yml) sources them as `vars.*` (non-secret GitHub Actions repository variables), reserving `secrets.*` only for JIRA_API_TOKEN. Deviating from this convention without justification forces operators to provision JIRA_URL and JIRA_USER in both vars (for reconcile-bridge) and secrets (for external-integration), and the AC3 'operator-attested' item also calls all three 'secrets', reinforcing the divergence.
findings.location: What section, item 1 ('map JIRA_URL/JIRA_USER/JIRA_API_TOKEN from secrets.*') and AC line 3 ('JIRA_URL/JIRA_USER/JIRA_API_TOKEN secrets')
findings.suggested_fix: In the What §1 description and AC3 wording, use `${{ vars.JIRA_URL }}` and `${{ vars.JIRA_USER }}` (repository variables) while keeping `${{ secrets.JIRA_API_TOKEN }}` as a secret — exactly mirroring reconcile-bridge.yml lines 87–89.

findings.finding: The plan says to map `JIRA_URL`/`JIRA_USER`/`JIRA_API_TOKEN` from `secrets.*`, but the established pattern in `reconcile-bridge.yml` maps non-sensitive `JIRA_URL` and `JIRA_USER` from `vars.*` (repo variables) and only the token from `secrets.*`. This inconsistency leaves the implementation and the operator-attested AC ambiguous about which GitHub store (Variables vs. Secrets) to use, risks the config-as-artifact test asserting the wrong binding, and unnecessarily obscures non-sensitive values in the secrets store.
findings.location: What §1 / AC line 1 / AC line 3 (operator-attested)
findings.suggested_fix: Align with `reconcile-bridge.yml`: map `JIRA_URL` and `JIRA_USER` from `vars.*` and only `JIRA_API_TOKEN` from `secrets.*`. Update the plan text in What §1 to read: 'map `JIRA_URL`/`JIRA_USER` from `vars.*` and `JIRA_API_TOKEN` from `secrets.*`'; update AC line 1 and the operator-attested AC line 3 to distinguish repo Variables from repo Secrets accordingly.

findings.finding: The plan maps JIRA_URL and JIRA_USER from `secrets.*` ('map `JIRA_URL`/`JIRA_USER`/`JIRA_API_TOKEN` from `secrets.*`'), but the established repo convention (reconcile-bridge.yml, jira-sync-setup.md) stores JIRA_URL and JIRA_USER as GitHub repo *Variables* (vars.*), not secrets. If the operator follows the existing documentation to configure these as Variables, the `secrets.JIRA_URL` and `secrets.JIRA_USER` references will resolve to empty strings, causing silent auth failure.
findings.location: What > Item 1: 'map `JIRA_URL`/`JIRA_USER`/`JIRA_API_TOKEN` from `secrets.*` into the external job's env'
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section and in the workflow YAML, use `JIRA_URL: ${{ vars.JIRA_URL }}`, `JIRA_USER: ${{ vars.JIRA_USER }}`, `JIRA_API_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.JIRA_API_TOKEN }}` — mirroring reconcile-bridge.yml lines 87-89 and 176-178.

findings.finding: The plan maps JIRA_URL, JIRA_USER, AND JIRA_API_TOKEN all from secrets.*, but JIRA_URL (a plain URL) and JIRA_USER (an email address) are non-sensitive and the repo's established pattern in reconcile-bridge.yml sources them from vars.JIRA_URL / vars.JIRA_USER (GitHub Actions repository variables), reserving secrets.* only for JIRA_API_TOKEN. Treating non-sensitive values as secrets violates least-privilege (over-classification), masks non-sensitive data in logs unnecessarily, and propagates the miscategorization into the operator-attested AC which says these 'secrets' must be configured in GitHub.
findings.location: What section item 1 ('map JIRA_URL/JIRA_USER/JIRA_API_TOKEN from secrets.*') and AC bullet 3 (operator-attested: 'JIRA_URL/JIRA_USER/JIRA_API_TOKEN secrets … are configured in the GitHub repo')
findings.suggested_fix: In What §1, change 'map JIRA_URL/JIRA_USER/JIRA_API_TOKEN from secrets.*' to 'map JIRA_URL from vars.JIRA_URL, JIRA_USER from vars.JIRA_USER, and JIRA_API_TOKEN from secrets.JIRA_API_TOKEN — matching the established convention in reconcile-bridge.yml'. In AC bullet 3, change 'JIRA_URL/JIRA_USER/JIRA_API_TOKEN secrets' to 'JIRA_URL/JIRA_USER repo variables and JIRA_API_TOKEN secret'.

findings.finding: The plan proposes mapping JIRA_URL and JIRA_USER from secrets.* but the existing repo workflow (reconcile-bridge.yml) stores them as vars.* (GitHub Actions repository variables, non-secret), with only JIRA_API_TOKEN as a secret. If the external-integration.yml implementation uses secrets.JIRA_URL and secrets.JIRA_USER while the values are registered as vars, the expressions will silently resolve to empty strings, causing the Jira tests to skip and the canary to remain a false green.
findings.location: What section, item 1 / Acceptance Criteria first bullet: 'maps JIRA_URL/JIRA_USER/JIRA_API_TOKEN from secrets into the external job env'
findings.suggested_fix: Mirror reconcile-bridge.yml: use `vars.JIRA_URL` and `vars.JIRA_USER` (non-sensitive) plus `secrets.JIRA_API_TOKEN` (sensitive). Update the plan's wording to 'map JIRA_URL / JIRA_USER from vars.* and JIRA_API_TOKEN from secrets.*', and update the config-as-artifact test to assert the correct expression forms.

findings.finding: The plan says to reuse the 'download+verify+install step' for acli from `reconcile-bridge.yml` but omits the companion 'Add acli to PATH and authenticate' step (reconcile-bridge.yml lines 174–185: `acli jira auth login --site … --email … --token`). Without authentication, `shutil.which('acli')` resolves to a binary (so `_live_jira_ready()` returns True and tests no longer skip), but every actual `acli` subprocess invocation fails at the auth layer. The plan's What §1 and AC bullet 1 only say 'installs the `acli` binary onto PATH' with no mention of the auth step.
findings.location: What §1 ('INSTALL the `acli` binary … adding `~/.acli` to PATH'); AC bullet 1 ('installs the `acli` binary onto PATH')
findings.suggested_fix: After the install step, add: `echo "$JIRA_API_TOKEN" | acli jira auth login --site "$JIRA_URL" --email "$JIRA_USER" --token` (mirroring reconcile-bridge.yml:180–185). Document this as a required step in the plan's What §1.

findings.finding: The plan specifies 'reusing the established download+verify+install step from reconcile-bridge.yml' for acli, but reconcile-bridge.yml also has a distinct 'Add acli to PATH and authenticate' step (lines 174-185) that runs `acli jira auth login` to persist session credentials. test_verified_fake_contract_live.py (line 15-17) explicitly notes that search_issues 'additionally needs an authenticated acli, not merely the binary on PATH.' Omitting the auth step leaves acli installed but unauthenticated, so any external test that shells out to acli (search_issues in test_verified_fake_contract_live.py) will still skip or error even with all env vars set.
findings.location: What section, item 1: acli install description
findings.suggested_fix: After the acli install step, add a step: `echo "$JIRA_API_TOKEN" | acli jira auth login --site "$JIRA_URL" --email "$JIRA_USER" --token` with the same env mapping, mirroring reconcile-bridge.yml lines 174-185.

findings.finding: The plan omits the `acli jira auth login` authentication step that reconcile-bridge.yml performs immediately after the binary install. The production AcliClient explicitly documents that 'ACLI does not read env vars for auth' and 'ACLI Go reads auth from its config file (set by `acli auth login`)' — so without this step, every acli subprocess call (create_issue, set_relationship, delete_issue) will fail with authentication errors even when JIRA_* env vars are set.
findings.location: What > Item 1: 'INSTALL the `acli` binary … adding `~/.acli` to PATH so `shutil.which("acli")` resolves'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a step identical to reconcile-bridge.yml's 'Add acli to PATH and authenticate' step (lines 174-185), mapping JIRA_URL/JIRA_USER from vars.* and JIRA_API_TOKEN from secrets.JIRA_API_TOKEN. Include this step in the What section's description of the workflow changes.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
